html:
[1011]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 選人 - Refers to a candidate for an official position, often a lower-ranking official.
  • 西頂社會 - Refers to a social gathering at the Western Peak, likely a place of religious or cultural significance.
  • 舉鼗一搖 - Means 'to shake the rattle drum once,' a gesture used to attract attention.
  • 毋多言 - Similar to '無多言,' meaning 'do not speak much.'
  • 我知爾 - Means 'I know you,' a statement of recognition or familiarity.
  • 不惧尔 - Means 'I am not afraid of you,' expressing confidence.
  • 闻尔辈能致财 - Means 'I have heard that you can bring wealth,' referring to the belief in supernatural abilities.
  • 若能赡我 - Means 'if you can support me,' a request for financial or material assistance.
  • 然则同归耳 - Means 'then we will go together,' indicating agreement.
  • 颇华洁 - Means 'quite elegant and clean,' describing the interior of the house.
  • 彼此意会 - Means 'we understand each other,' indicating a mutual understanding without words.
  • 惟献酬款洽而已 - Means 'we only exchange gifts and are friendly,' describing the interaction between the two.
  • 酒阑就宿 - Means 'when the wine is finished, we go to bed,' indicating the end of the evening and the start of the night.
  • 备极燕婉 - Means 'to the utmost of intimacy,' describing the intimate relationship.
  • 次日入城 - Means 'the next day, we enter the city,' indicating the continuation of their journey.
  • 携小奴及眂被往 - Means 'taking a small servant and a blanket with him,' describing the items he brought.
  • 渐使拂枕簟 - Means 'gradually making the bed,' indicating the preparation for sleep.
  • 侍梳沐 - Means 'to serve in washing and grooming,' describing the care provided.
  • 理衣裳 - Means 'to arrange clothes,' indicating the preparation for the next day.
  • 司洒扫 - Means 'to take care of cleaning,' describing the domestic duties.
  • 烟筒茗碗之役 - Means 'the tasks of tending to the stove and tea bowl,' indicating the daily chores.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 图裕斋前辈 - Refers to a respected elder or mentor, likely a figure of authority or wisdom.
  • 钓鱼台 - Refers to a specific location, possibly a scenic spot or a place of historical significance.
  • 薄暮车马渐稀 - Describes the scene at dusk when the number of vehicles and horses decreases, indicating the end of the day.
  • 一女子左抱小儿，右持鼗鼓 - Describes a woman holding a child and a rattle drum, a common sight in traditional Chinese settings.
  • 袅袅来 - Describes the woman's graceful approach.
  • 选人一笑，女子亦一笑 - Describes the mutual smiles between the man and the woman, indicating a friendly exchange.
  • 选人故狡黠 - Describes the man as cunning or clever, suggesting a strategic approach.
  • 揣女子装束类贵家 - Means 'he guessed that the woman's attire was like that of a noble family,' indicating a perception of her status.
  • 而抱子独行，又似村妇 - Means 'but she was carrying a child alone, which seemed like a village woman,' suggesting a contrast in appearance.
  • 踪迹诡异 - Means 'her movements were strange,' indicating a sense of mystery or suspicion.
  • 疑为狐魅 - Means 'he suspected her to be a fox spirit,' a reference to supernatural beliefs.
  • 因逐之絮谈 - Means 'he followed her and engaged in a long conversation,' indicating a prolonged interaction.
  • 女子微露夫亡之幼意 - Means 'the woman subtly revealed her young widowhood,' suggesting a personal story.
  • 选人笑语之曰 - Means 'the man smiled and said to her,' indicating a friendly and reassuring tone.
  • 毋多言，我知尔，亦不惧尔，然我贫，闻尔辈能致财，若能赡我，我即从尔去 - The man's statement, indicating his understanding and request for support.
  • 女子亦笑曰 - Means 'the woman also smiled and said,' indicating a positive response.
  • 然则同归耳 - Means 'then we will go together,' indicating agreement.
  • 至其家屋，不甚宏壮，而颇华洁 - Describes the house as not very grand but elegant and clean.
  • 亦有父母姑姐妹 - Means 'she also had parents, aunts, and sisters,' indicating a family setting.
  • 彼此意会，不复话氏族 - Means 'they understood each other without talking about family backgrounds,' indicating a mutual understanding.
  • 惟献酬款洽而已 - Means 'they only exchanged gifts and were friendly,' describing the interaction.
  • 酒阑就宿 - Means 'when the wine was finished, they went to bed,' indicating the end of the evening.
  • 备极燕婉 - Means 'to the utmost of intimacy,' describing the intimate relationship.
  • 次日入城 - Means 'the next day, they entered the city,' indicating the continuation of their journey.
  • 携小奴及眂被往 - Means 'taking a small servant and a blanket with him,' describing the items he brought.
  • 颇相安 - Means 'they were quite comfortable,' indicating a settled state.
  • 惟女子冶荡无度 - Means 'the woman was wanton without limit,' describing her behavior.
  • 奔命殆疲 - Means 'running around almost exhausted,' indicating the physical strain.
  • 又渐使拂枕簟 - Means 'gradually making the bed,' indicating the preparation for sleep.
  • 侍梳沐 - Means 'to serve in washing and grooming,' describing the care provided.
  • 理衣裳 - Means 'to arrange clothes,' indicating the preparation for the next day.
  • 司洒扫 - Means 'to take care of cleaning,' describing the domestic duties.
  • 至于烟筒茗碗之役，亦遣执之 - Means 'even the tasks of tending to the stove and tea bowl were also assigned to her,' indicating the daily chores.

[1012]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 久而 - Temporal phrase meaning 'after a long time' or 'eventually.'
  • 调谑指挥 - Means 'teasing and commanding,' describing a dismissive and authoritative attitude.
  • 视如僮婢 - Means 'to treat like servants,' emphasizing a degrading relationship.
  • 选人耽其色，利其财 - Means 'the man was attracted by her beauty and tempted by her wealth,' highlighting his motivations.
  • 不能拒也 - Means 'could not refuse,' indicating his helplessness.
  • 使涤厕硄 - Means 'to make him clean the toilet,' a demeaning task.
  • 事事随汝意，此乃不随我意耶 - Means 'Everything follows your wishes, is this not following mine?' a rhetorical question expressing frustration.
  • 诸女亦助之诮责 - Means 'the other women also joined in mocking and scolding,' indicating collective derision.
  • 渐相忤 - Means 'gradually became at odds,' describing the growing tension.
  • 夜出不归 - Means 'went out at night and did not return,' suggesting secretive behavior.
  • 亲戚留宿 - Means 'stayed overnight with relatives,' a plausible but suspicious excuse.
  • 中表 - Means 'cousins' or 'relatives,' used here to describe visitors.
  • 嬉笑燕饮 - Means 'laughing, joking, and drinking,' describing a lively but possibly inappropriate atmosphere.
  • 琵琶度曲 - Means 'playing the pipa and singing,' indicating musical entertainment.
  • 禁选人勿至前 - Means 'forbade the man from coming near,' showing exclusion.
  • 恚愤 - Means 'furious,' expressing intense anger.
  • 不如是，金帛从何来 - Means 'If not like this, where would the gold and silk come from?' a justification for her actions.
  • 使我谢客易 - Means 'If I were to refuse guests, it would be easy,' a sarcastic remark.
  • 一家三十口，须汝供给，汝能之耶 - Means 'We have thirty people in our family, can you provide for us?' a challenge to his ability.
  • 知不可留 - Means 'knew he could not stay,' indicating his resignation.
  • 携小奴入京 - Means 'took a small servant to the capital,' describing his departure.
  • 僦住屋 - Means 'rented a house,' indicating his new living arrangement.
  • 荒烟蔓草 - Means 'wild grass and smoke,' describing the desolation of the place.
  • 无复人居 - Means 'no one lives there anymore,' emphasizing the emptiness.
  • 衣装不知所往矣 - Means 'the clothes are unknown where they went,' indicating loss or disappearance.
  • 本携数百金 - Means 'originally brought hundreds of gold,' describing his initial wealth.
  • 善治生 - Means 'good at managing wealth,' indicating his financial skills.
  • 衣颇褴缕 - Means 'clothes were quite tattered,' describing his current state.
  • 忽被服华楚 - Means 'suddenly dressed in fine and elegant clothes,' indicating a sudden change.
  • 具言赘婿状 - Means 'explained the situation of a son-in-law,' describing his status.
  • 讳不自言 - Means 'was reluctant to speak about it himself,' indicating secrecy.
  • 小奴私泄其事 - Means 'the small servant secretly revealed the matter,' leading to the truth being known.
  • 人乃知之 - Means 'people then knew it,' indicating the revelation.
  • 此魅窃逃 - Means 'this demon secretly fled,' a metaphorical description of her disappearance.
  • 犹有人理 - Means 'still had some human reason,' indicating that her actions were somewhat rational.
  • 吾所见有甚于此者矣 - Means 'I have seen things even worse than this,' indicating a comparison to other experiences.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 选人 - Refers to a man who has been chosen or selected, possibly a reference to a specific social or political context.
  • 女子 - Refers to a woman, likely a wife or a mistress.
  • 亲戚 - Refers to relatives, a common social structure in Chinese culture.
  • 中表 - Refers to cousins or extended family, indicating a broad network of kinship.
  • 琵琶度曲 - Refers to playing the pipa and singing, a traditional form of entertainment.
  • 金帛 - Refers to gold and silk, symbols of wealth and luxury.
  • 治生 - Refers to managing wealth, a practical skill in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 曹慕堂宗丞 - Refers to a specific person, likely a historical figure or a contemporary observer, who provides commentary on the situation.

[1013]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 武强张公令誉 - Zhang Gong from Wuqiang with a good reputation.
  • 康熙丁酉举人 - Passed the imperial examination in the year Dingyou of the Kangxi era.
  • 刘景南之妇翁也 - Is Liu Jingnan's father-in-law.
  • 纳一姬 - Married a concubine.
  • 聘币颇轻 - The dowry was quite light.
  • 每月当十五日在寓 - Should be at the residence on the fifteenth day of each month.
  • 归宁 - Return to her parents' home.
  • 悦其色美而值廉 - Pleased by her beauty and the low cost.
  • 竟曲从之 - Finally complied with it.
  • 约亦如是 - Agreed to do the same.
  • 询访信然 - Inquired and found it to be true.
  • 一人也 - It was the same person.
  • 盖其初鬻之时，已预留再鬻地矣 - Because when she was first sold, they had already reserved the possibility of reselling her.
  • 张公淳实君子 - Zhang Gong is a sincere and honest gentleman.
  • 度必无妄言 - He must have no false words.
  • 京师鬻女之家 - Families in the capital who sell women.
  • 虽变幻万状 - Even if they change in countless ways.
  • 欺以其方 - Deceive them with their own methods.
  • 月月克日归宁 - Return to her parents' home on a fixed date every month.
  • 不近事理 - Not reasonable or logical.
  • 不时往来于两家 - Frequent travel between the two families.
  • 岂人不能闻 - How could people not know?
  • 是必败之道 - This is surely a path to failure.
  • 狡黠者断不出此 - Cunning people would never do this.
  • 或传闻失实 - Perhaps the hearsay was inaccurate.
  • 张公误听之欤 - Did Zhang Gong mishear it?
  • 紫陌看花，动多迷路 - Even when walking on the purple path to admire flowers, one often gets lost.
  • 其造作是语 - The creation of this story.
  • 固亦不为无因耳 - Indeed, it is not without reason.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 武强 - A county in Hebei Province, China.
  • 康熙 - The reign title of Emperor Kangxi.
  • 举人 - A title given to those who passed the provincial-level imperial examination in the Chinese civil service system.
  • 妇翁 - Father-in-law, a term used in traditional Chinese family relationships.
  • 纳一姬 - Married a concubine, a practice common in traditional Chinese society.
  • 聘币 - Dowry, the payment made by the groom's family to the bride's family.
  • 归宁 - A term used for a woman returning to her parents' home, especially during the first month after marriage.
  • 京师 - Capital city, referring to Beijing during the Qing dynasty.
  • 紫陌 - Imperial road, a term often used in classical Chinese literature to refer to the main roads leading to the imperial city.

Proper Nouns

  • 武强 - A county in Hebei Province, China.
  • 张公 - Zhang Gong, a person with a good reputation.
  • 刘景南 - Liu Jingnan, a person whose father-in-law is Zhang Gong.
  • 康熙 - The reign title of Emperor Kangxi.
  • 丁酉 - The year 1717, during the reign of Emperor Kangxi.
  • 举人 - A title given to those who passed the provincial-level imperial examination.
  • 妇翁 - Father-in-law.
  • 纳一姬 - Married a concubine.
  • 聘币 - Dowry.
  • 归宁 - Return to her parents' home.
  • 京师 - Capital city, referring to Beijing during the Qing dynasty.
  • 紫陌 - Imperial road.

[1014]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以五百金纳一姬 - Means 'to purchase a concubine for five hundred taels of silver.'
  • 大骇愕 - Means 'greatly startled' or 'shocked.'
  • 恐误认 - Means 'fearing a mistaken recognition.'
  • 仓皇不知所对 - Means 'in a state of panic and unable to respond.'
  • 嗒然无一语 - Means 'silent and speechless.'
  • 深自引愆 - Means 'deeply admitting one's fault.'
  • 具述方鬻次女 - Means 'to explain that they were about to sell their second daughter.'
  • 借衣随媒媪同往事 - Means 'borrowed clothing and followed the matchmaker on the journey.'
  • 无心凑合 - Means 'an accidental meeting' or 'a fortuitous encounter.'
  • 佳话 - Means 'a beautiful story' or 'a delightful tale.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 李华麓 - The name of a man who is the protagonist of the story.
  • 天津 - A city in northern China, known historically for its significance.
  • 媒媪 - Refers to a matchmaker, typically an older woman.
  • 绣衫 - Refers to a finely embroidered robe, often worn by women of higher social status.
  • 风水相遭 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'meeting by chance' or 'accidental encounter.'

Proper Nouns

  • 李华麓 - The name of the protagonist.
  • 天津 - The city where the protagonist met the friend.
  • 媒媪 - The matchmakers involved in the story.
  • 绣衫 - The embroidered robe that caused the confusion.

[1015]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 狂生 - Refers to a wild or eccentric scholar, often used to describe someone who is unconventional or rebellious.
  • 性悖妄 - Means 'having a perverse and deluded nature,' describing someone who is irrational or unreasonable.
  • 诋訾今古 - Means 'to criticize both ancient and modern times,' indicating a tendency to criticize everything without distinction.
  • 高自位置 - Means 'to position oneself as superior,' suggesting a sense of arrogance or self-importance.
  • 衔之次骨 - Means 'to harbor deep resentment,' indicating intense anger or hatred.
  • 值河间岁试 - Means 'during the annual examination in Hejian,' referring to a specific event in the text.
  • 同寓十数人 - Means 'several dozen people staying in the same lodging,' describing a group of people.
  • 或相识，或不相识 - Means 'some are acquainted, others are not,' indicating a mix of familiar and unfamiliar people.
  • 夏夜散坐庭院纳凉 - Means 'sitting scattered in the courtyard on a summer night to cool off,' describing a scene of relaxation.
  • 纵意高谈 - Means 'to speak freely and loftily,' indicating unrestrained and elevated speech.
  • 众畏其唇吻 - Means 'everyone fears his words,' indicating that people are intimidated by his speech.
  • 皆缄口不答 - Means 'all remain silent and do not respond,' describing the reaction of the audience.
  • 惟树后坐一人 - Means 'only one person sitting behind a tree,' indicating a specific individual.
  • 抗词与辩 - Means 'to argue back with words,' describing a counterargument.
  • 连抵其隙 - Means 'to repeatedly find and exploit his weaknesses,' indicating a skillful counterargument.
  • 理屈词穷 - Means 'to have no logical basis and run out of words,' describing a state of being unable to defend one's position.
  • 暗中应曰 - Means 'to respond in the dark,' indicating a secretive or hidden response.
  • 仆焦王相也 - Means 'I am Jiao Wangxiang,' a reference to a specific person.
  • 河间之宿儒 - Means 'an old scholar in Hejian,' describing Jiao Wangxiang's status.
  • 骇问子不久死耶 - Means 'shocked to ask if you won't die soon,' indicating a surprise or disbelief.
  • 笑应曰 - Means 'to respond with a laugh,' describing a humorous reaction.
  • 仆如不死，敢捋虎须耶 - Means 'if I don't die, how dare I touch the tiger's whiskers,' a metaphor for daring to challenge a powerful person.
  • 狂生跳掷叫号 - Means 'the wild scholar jumps and shouts,' describing a physical and vocal reaction.
  • 绕墙寻觅 - Means 'to search around the wall,' indicating a frantic search.
  • 惟闻笑声吃吃 - Means 'only to hear laughter,' describing the sound of laughter.
  • 或在木桫，或在檐端而已 - Means 'sometimes in the tree, sometimes at the eaves,' indicating the elusive nature of the laughter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 河间 - Refers to Hejian, a historical region in present-day Hebei Province, China.
  • 岁试 - Refers to the annual examinations held during the imperial examination system.
  • 焦王相 - Refers to Jiao Wangxiang, an old scholar in Hejian, whose status and reputation are mentioned in the text.
  • 宿儒 - Refers to an old scholar or a person of great learning and experience.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 狂生纵意高谈，众畏其唇吻，皆缄口不答 - This sentence describes the wild scholar's unrestrained speech and the audience's fear and silence. The structure is clear but requires careful rendering to maintain the tension and atmosphere.
  • 惟树后坐一人，抗词与辩，连抵其隙，理屈词穷 - This sentence introduces a specific individual who counters the wild scholar's arguments, leading to the wild scholar's defeat. The sequence of actions is clear but needs to be rendered in a way that maintains the narrative flow.
  • 暗中应曰：仆焦王相也，河间之宿儒 - This sentence introduces Jiao Wangxiang, whose identity and status are revealed in a secretive manner. The structure is clear but requires careful rendering to maintain the element of surprise.
  • 骇问子不久死耶？笑应曰：仆如不死，敢捋虎须耶 - This sentence describes the wild scholar's reaction to Jiao Wangxiang's revelation, including his shock and the subsequent humorous response. The structure is clear but requires careful rendering to maintain the emotional and comedic elements.
  • 狂生跳掷叫号，绕墙寻觅，惟闻笑声吃吃，或在木桫，或在檐端而已 - This sentence describes the wild scholar's frantic search and the elusive nature of the laughter. The structure is clear but requires careful rendering to maintain the sense of mystery and humor.

Proper Nouns

  • 河间 - The historical region of Hejian.
  • 焦王相 - The name of the old scholar, Jiao Wangxiang.
  • 宿儒 - The title of an old scholar, used to describe Jiao Wangxiang.

[1016]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 力若不胜 - Describes the woman's physical state, seeming to be struggling.
  • 调谑 - Describes the young man's flirtatious behavior.
  • 姑贳尔 - A conditional pardon offered to the young man.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王洪绪 - A historical figure or storyteller, whose name provides a source for the narrative.
  • 鄚州 - A place name, indicating the location of the story.
  • 筑堤 - Refers to the construction of an embankment, a common activity in ancient times.
  • 秫田 - Refers to a millet field, a type of agricultural land.
  • 狐穴被水 - Refers to a flooded fox den, a supernatural element.

Proper Nouns

  • 王洪绪 - The narrator or storyteller.
  • 鄚州 - The location of the story.
  • 少妇 - The woman in the story.
  • 幼弟 - The woman's younger brother.
  • 少年 - The young man who accompanies the woman.
  • 塍 - The ditch that needs to be dug.
  • 锸 - The spade used for digging.
  • 狐穴 - The fox den that is suspected to be flooded.

[1017]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 姑妄聽之 - Phrase meaning 'listen to it casually' or 'take it with a grain of salt'; indicates the story is not to be taken too seriously.
  • 依其墓以居 - Phrase meaning 'residing near the grave' or 'living by the side of the grave'; indicates a close association with the deceased.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 文安 - Place name referring to Wen'an County in Hebei Province; historically significant.
  • 古北口 - Historically important pass in northern China; known for its strategic military significance.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains of China; often associated with longevity and immortality.
  • 密雲 - Miyun County, historically known for its natural resources and scenic beauty.
  • 狐女 - Fox spirit in Chinese folklore; often depicted as a beautiful woman who can transform into a fox.

Proper Nouns

  • 族侄竹汀 - Specific individual, likely a cousin or relative named Zhuting.

[1018]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初甚骇怖 - 初 means 'at first,' and 骇怖 means 'frightened or terrified.' This phrase describes an initial state of fear.
  • 既而见其意真切 - 既而 means 'soon after' or 'after a while,' and 意真切 means 'the intention is sincere.' This phrase indicates a change in perception after some time.
  • 相持涕泣 - 相持 means 'holding each other,' and 涕泣 means 'crying.' This phrase describes an emotional embrace with tears.
  • 奉事无不至 - 奉事 means 'to serve' or 'to attend to,' and 无不至 means 'without fail' or 'completely.' This phrase indicates that the fox woman served without fail.
  • 转胜于有子 - 转胜 means 'to surpass' or 'to outdo,' and 有子 means 'having a son.' This phrase suggests that the fox woman's service was better than having a human son.
  • 遣老妪市一棺 - 遣 means 'to send,' 老妪 means 'an old woman,' and 市 means 'to buy.' This phrase describes sending an old woman to buy a coffin.
  • 具锸畚 - 具 means 'to prepare,' 锸 means 'a spade,' and 畚 means 'a basket.' This phrase indicates preparing tools for digging.
  • 翁姑宜贺儿 - 翁姑 refers to 'husband and wife' or 'parents-in-law,' and 宜贺 means 'should celebrate.' This phrase suggests that the husband and wife should celebrate their daughter.
  • 奉事翁姑，自追念逝者，聊尽寸心耳 - 奉事翁姑 means 'to serve the husband and wife,' 自追念逝者 means 'to remember the deceased,' and 聊尽寸心耳 means 'just to express a small part of one's heart.' This phrase explains the fox woman's motivation for her actions.
  • 感动土神，闻于岳帝 - 感动土神 means 'to move the local deities,' and 闻于岳帝 means 'to be heard by the Lord of Mount Tai.' This phrase indicates that the fox woman's sincerity moved the local deities and reached the Lord of Mount Tai.
  • 许不待丹成，解形证果 - 许 means 'to permit,' 不待丹成 means 'without waiting for the elixir to be completed,' and 解形证果 means 'to transcend physical form and achieve enlightenment.' This phrase suggests that the Lord of Mount Tai permitted the fox woman to achieve enlightenment without waiting for the elixir to be completed.
  • 遗蜕合窆 - 遗蜕 means 'the remains of the physical body,' and 合窆 means 'to be buried together.' This phrase describes the fox woman's remains being buried together with her husband.
  • 隶碧霞元君为女官 - 隶 means 'to belong to,' 碧霞元君 is the title of a female deity, and 为女官 means 'to be a female official.' This phrase indicates that the fox woman became a female official under the Goddess of Mount Tai.
  • 僦屋与土人杂居 - 僦屋 means 'to rent a house,' and 与土人杂居 means 'to live among the locals.' This phrase describes the fox woman renting a house and living among the locals.
  • 无所为而为 - 无所为 means 'without any purpose,' and 而为 means 'to do.' This phrase suggests that the fox woman's actions were done without any ulterior motive.
  • 无不忠不孝之神仙 - 无不忠不孝 means 'there are no disloyal or unfilial,' and 神仙 means 'immortals.' This phrase suggests that there are no immortals who are disloyal or unfilial.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐女 - A fox spirit, often associated with supernatural abilities and transformations in Chinese folklore.
  • 碧霞元君 - The Goddess of Mount Tai, a prominent deity in Chinese folk religion, often associated with protection and fertility.
  • 岳帝 - The Lord of Mount Tai, a high-ranking deity in Chinese mythology, often associated with justice and the afterlife.
  • 丹成 - The completion of an elixir of immortality, a common theme in Chinese alchemy and Daoism.
  • 遗蜕 - The remains of the physical body after achieving spiritual transformation, a concept found in Daoist and Buddhist traditions.

Proper Nouns

  • 狐女 - The fox spirit.
  • 碧霞元君 - The Goddess of Mount Tai.
  • 岳帝 - The Lord of Mount Tai.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Chinese culture.

[1019]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 竹汀又言 - Narrative marker meaning 'Zhudting further said.'

  • 司爨王媪言 - Formal phrase meaning 'the old woman who manages the kitchen (司爨王媪) says.'
  • 即见醉钟馗首 - Vivid description of seeing a drunken Zhong Kui's head. '醉' means 'drunken,' and '钟馗首' refers to the head of Zhong Kui, a legendary figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 力倦小憩 - Literally 'tired and taking a short rest,' a common phrase in Classical Chinese to describe a brief pause after exertion.
  • 履险阻如坦途 - Metaphor meaning 'to tread dangerous obstacles as if they were smooth paths,' indicating someone who moves effortlessly through difficulties.

  • 辗转倚伏，机械又安有穷欤 - Means 'endless cycles of dependence and deceit, how can such schemes ever end?'

  • 心计万万不到此 - Means 'its cunning and calculation are far from reaching this level.'
  • 闻伥役于虎，必得代乃转生 - Means 'it is said that the ghost serves the tiger and can only be reborn after finding a replacement.'
  • 是殆伥诱人自代，因引人捕虎报冤也 - Means 'it is likely that the ghost lures people to replace them, thereby leading them to hunt and kill the tiger to avenge themselves.'
  • 伥者人所化 - Means 'the ghost is a human who has transformed.'
  • 揆诸人事，固亦有之 - Means 'considering human affairs, such things do indeed exist.'
  • 又惜虎知伥助己，不知即伥害己矣 - Means 'it is a pity that the tiger knows the ghost helps it but does not realize that the ghost is also harming it.'

  • 伥鬼 - A ghost that serves a tiger and helps it find prey.
  • 虎 - The tiger is a symbol of strength and ferocity in Chinese culture, often associated with danger and the supernatural.
  • 铳 - A firearm, indicating a modern or semi-modern context within the narrative.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 司爨王媪言——即见醉钟馗首 —— The dash introduces a vivid description of what the old woman saw.
  • 有樵者，伐木山冈，力倦小憩 —— This sentence describes a woodcutter who is tired and takes a break.
  • 遥见一人持衣数袭，沿路弃之 —— The woodcutter sees a person carrying several sets of clothes and discarding them along the way.
  • 不省其何故，谛视之，履险阻如坦途，其行甚速，非人可及 —— The woodcutter is puzzled by the behavior and observes the person moving swiftly through dangerous terrain.
  • 貌亦惨淡不似人，疑为妖魅，登高树瞰之，人已不见 —— The person's appearance is ghostly, leading the woodcutter to suspect it is a demon or ghost.
  • 由其弃衣之路，宛转至山坳，则一虎伏焉 —— Following the path of discarded clothes, the woodcutter finds a tiger lying in a valley.
  • 知人为伥鬼，衣所食者之遗也 —— The woodcutter realizes the person is a ghost and the clothes are from those the tiger has eaten.
  • 急弃柴，自冈后遁 —— The woodcutter quickly abandons his wood and flees down the hill.
  • 次日，闻某村某甲，于是地死于虎矣 —— The next day, the woodcutter hears that someone from a village died there at the hands of the tiger.
  • 路非人径所必经，知其以衣为饵，导之至是也 —— The path is not a common route, suggesting the ghost lured the person there with the clothes.
  • 物莫灵于人，人恒以饵取物，今物乃以饵取人，岂人弗灵哉 —— This philosophical reflection contrasts the intelligence of humans with their susceptibility to being lured.
  • 利汨其灵，故智出物下耳 —— Greed obscures human wisdom, making them less intelligent than animals.
  • 合铳群击，殪其三焉 —— The hunters use firearms to kill three tigers.
  • 辗转倚伏，机械又安有穷欤 —— The endless cycle of dependency and deceit suggests that such schemes will never end.
  • 或又曰 —— Introduces an alternative explanation.
  • 虎至悍而至愚 —— Describes the tiger as both fierce and foolish.
  • 心计万万不到此 —— Indicates that the tiger's cunning is far below that of humans.
  • 闻伥役于虎，必得代乃转生 —— Explains the belief that the ghost serves the tiger and can only be reborn after finding a replacement.
  • 是殆伥诱人自代，因引人捕虎报冤也 —— Suggests that the ghost lures people to replace them, leading to the tiger's demise.
  • 伥者人所化 —— Indicates that the ghost is a human who has transformed.
  • 揆诸人事，固亦有之 —— Reflects on the existence of such phenomena in human affairs.
  • 又惜虎知伥助己，不知即伥害己矣 —— Expresses pity that the tiger does not realize the ghost is also harming it.

Proper Nouns

  • 司爨王媪 - The old woman who manages the kitchen.
  • 钟馗 - A legendary figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 伥鬼 - A ghost that serves a tiger.
  • 虎 - The tiger, a symbol of strength and danger.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 从侄秀山言 - Common way of introducing a story or anecdote; 'From my cousin Xiu Shan's account' or 'According to my cousin Xiu Shan.'

  • 甫出栅 - 'As soon as he left the fence/gate'; '甫' indicates immediacy, and '栅' is a wooden fence or gate.

  • 投井佳 - 'Throwing oneself into a well is good'; a literal translation, but in context, it’s a suggestion from a ghost.
  • 自缢更佳 - 'Hanging oneself is even better'; again, a suggestion from a ghost.
  • 左右牵制，莫知所适 - 'On both sides, he was restrained; he did not know where to go'; a state of confusion or indecision.
  • 俄有旧识丁文奎者从北来 - 'Soon, an old acquaintance named Ding Wenkui came from the north'; a complex sentence structure typical in classical Chinese.
  • 挥拳击二鬼遁去 - 'He struck the two ghosts with his fist, causing them to flee'; a vivid action verb.
  • 而自送士俊归 - 'And then he sent Shi Jun back home'; '送' can mean 'to send' or 'to escort.'
  • 惘惘如梦醒 - 'Wandering as if waking from a dream'; a metaphor for confusion or disorientation.
  • 自尽之心顿息 - 'His desire to commit suicide suddenly ceased'; a clear statement of emotional resolution.
  • 盖二人同役于叔父栗甫公家 - 'For the two of them had served together in the household of my uncle Li Fu'; '盖' introduces an explanation.
  • 文奎殁后，其母婴疾困卧 - 'After Wenkui died, his wife and child were ill and bedridden'; a statement of hardship.
  • 士俊尝助以钱五百 - 'Shi Jun once helped them with five hundred coins'; '尝' indicates a past action.
  • 故以是报之 - 'Therefore, he repaid them in this way'; a conclusion to the cause-effect relationship.
  • 此余家近岁事 - 'This is an event from my family in recent years'; a clear reference to the speaker's personal experience.
  • 与新齐谐所记针工遇鬼略相似 - 'This is similar to the story recorded in *Xin Qi Xie* about a needleworker encountering a ghost'; a reference to a classical text.
  • 信凿然有之 - 'It is indeed a verifiable and solid occurrence'; '凿然' means 'clearly, certainly.'
  • 而文奎之求代而来，报恩而去 - 'And Wenkui came to take his place and departed after repaying the debt'; a moral commentary on gratitude and sacrifice.
  • 尤足以激薄俗矣 - 'This is especially sufficient to inspire or move the shallow and worldly'; '激' means 'to inspire' or 'to move,' and '薄俗' refers to superficial or worldly people.

  • 露刃跃过 - *露刃* means to unsheathe or display a blade; *跃过* is to jump or leap over.
  • 尽驱妇女出舱外 - *尽驱* means to drive out completely; *出舱外* is to go outside the cabin or compartment.
  • 靓妆女子隔窗指一小妇曰 - *靓妆* means 'well-dressed' or 'beautifully adorned'; *隔窗* is 'from across the window.'
  • 此即是矣 - A direct and forceful statement.
  • 群盗应声曳之去 - *应声* is 'in response to the sound' or 'immediately'; *曳之去* is 'dragged her away.'
  • 一盗大呼曰 - *大呼* is 'to shout loudly.'
  • 我即尔家某婢父 - *某婢父* identifies the speaker as the father of a servant girl, though the name is omitted.
  • 尔女酷虐我女 - *酷虐* is a strong term meaning 'severe cruelty' or 'torture.'
  • 鞭箠炮烙无人理 - *鞭箠* refers to whipping and beating; *炮烙* is a form of punishment involving branding with hot iron.
  • 幸逃出遇我 - *幸* is 'fortunately'; *逃出* is 'escaped'; *遇我* is 'met me.'
  • 尔追捕未获，衔冤次骨 - *衔冤* is 'to hold a grudge'; *次骨* is a metaphor for deep in the bones or deeply felt.
  • 今来复仇也 - *复仇* is 'to take revenge.'
  • 言讫，扬帆顺流去 - *言讫* is 'after speaking'; *扬帆* is 'to raise the sail'; *顺流* is 'downstream.'
  • 斯须灭影 - *斯须* is 'a short while'; *灭影* is 'the shadow disappeared.'
  • 缉寻无迹 - *缉寻* is 'to search for'; *无迹* is 'no trace.'
  • 女竟不知其所终 - *竟* is 'ultimately'; *所终* is 'where she ended up.'
  • 然情状可想矣 - *情状* is 'the situation'; *可想* is 'can be imagined.'
  • 夫贫至鬻女 - *鬻女* is 'to sell a daughter' (a reference to selling a daughter into servitude or marriage).
  • 岂复有所能为 - *岂复* is 'how could they even'; *有所能为* is 'have the ability to do anything.'
  • 而不虑其能为盗也 - *不虑* is 'did not consider'; *能为盗* is 'could become a bandit.'
  • 婢受惨毒 - *惨毒* is 'cruel and harsh treatment.'
  • 岂复能报 - *岂复* again is 'how could she even.'
  • 此所谓蜂虿有毒欤 - *蜂虿* are scorpions and wasps, used metaphorically to refer to small creatures with deadly venom; *有毒* is 'has poison.'
  • 又李受公言 - *李受公* is another storyteller or recorder, similar to *周景垣前辈*.
  • 有御婢残忍者 - *御婢* is a palace servant or concubine; *残忍* is 'cruel.'
  • 偶以小过闭空房 - *偶* is 'once'; *小过* is 'a minor offense'; *闭空房* is 'locked in an empty room.'
  • 冻饿死 - *冻饿* is 'froze and starved'; *死* is 'died.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周景垣前辈 - *Zhou Jingyuan* is a name of a person, likely a scholar or storyteller from the Qing or earlier period. *前辈* means 'elder' or 'senior,' indicating a respected figure or storyteller.
  • 巨室眷属 - *巨室* refers to a large or prominent household; *眷属* is 'family members' or 'relatives.'
  • 连舻之任 - *连舻* is a rare phrase referring to boats tied together in a line, suggesting a journey by water.
  • 门灯樯帜 - *门灯* and *樯帜* are specific to boats, indicating that the vessel is well-equipped and possibly of official or military status.
  • 官舫 - An *official boat*, used by government officials or for official travel.
  • 露刃 - *Exposing the blade*, a dramatic and vivid description of armed men.
  • 鞭箠炮烙 - These are all forms of punishment. *鞭箠* is whipping; *炮烙* is a form of torture where the victim is burned with hot iron.
  • 衔冤次骨 - *Carrying grudge to the bone*, a metaphor for deep, lingering resentment.
  • 蜂虿有毒 - A classical idiom from *Zhuangzi* or other texts, meaning even small creatures can be dangerous. Used metaphorically to say that even those in low positions can be vengeful or dangerous.
  • 御婢 - *A palace maid* or *concubine*, indicating someone in the service of the imperial court.
  • 闭空房 - *Locking someone in an empty room* was a form of punishment in ancient China, often used against servants or concubines.

[1047]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然无伤痕 - Literally means 'yet without wounds,' used to contrast the absence of visible injuries with the underlying injustice.
  • 其父讼不得直 - Means 'his father's lawsuit did not receive justice,' where '直' refers to fairness or justice.
  • 反受笞 - Means 'instead, he was beaten,' where '笞' is a form of corporal punishment.
  • 冤愤莫释 - Means 'grievance and anger could not be relieved,' where '莫释' means 'cannot be alleviated.'
  • 夜逾垣入 - Means 'at night, he climbed over the wall to enter,' where '逾垣' is a specific action of climbing over a wall.
  • 并其母女手刃之 - Means 'he killed his mother and daughter with his own hands,' where '手刃之' emphasizes the personal and brutal nature of the act.
  • 缉捕多年，竟终漏网 - Means 'after being pursued for many years, he eventually escaped,' where '漏网' means 'to escape the net' or 'to evade capture.'
  • 是不为盗亦能报矣 - Means 'this shows that even if one is not a thief, one can still take revenge,' where '报' means 'to take revenge.'
  • 又言京师某家火 - Means 'it is also said that a house in the capital city caught fire,' where '火' means 'fire.'
  • 夫妇子女并焚 - Means 'the husband, wife, and children were all burned,' where '并焚' means 'all burned together.'
  • 亦群婢怨毒之所为 - Means 'it was also the result of the resentment and hatred of the maids,' where '怨毒' means 'resentment and hatred.'
  • 事无显证 - Means 'there was no clear evidence,' where '显证' means 'clear evidence.'
  • 遂无可追求 - Means 'so there was nothing to pursue,' where '追求' means 'to pursue.'
  • 是不必有父，亦自能报矣 - Means 'this shows that even without a father, one can still take revenge,' where '报' again means 'to take revenge.'
  • 余有亲串 - Means 'I have relatives and acquaintances,' where '亲串' means 'relatives and acquaintances.'
  • 鞭笞婢妾，嬉笑如儿戏 - Means 'whipping the maids and concubines, laughing and joking like a child's game,' where '鞭笞' means 'whipping' and '嬉笑如儿戏' means 'laughing and joking like a child's game.'
  • 间有死者 - Means 'there were occasional deaths,' where '间有' means 'occasionally.'
  • 一夕有黑气如车轮，自檐堕下，旋转如风 - Means 'one night, a black mist like a cartwheel fell from the eaves, spinning like the wind,' where '车轮' means 'cartwheel' and '旋转如风' means 'spinning like the wind.'
  • 啾啾然有声，直入内室而隐 - Means 'making a chirping sound, it went straight into the inner room and disappeared,' where '啾啾然' means 'chirping' and '直入内室而隐' means 'went straight into the inner room and disappeared.'
  • 次日，疽发于项如粟颗，渐以四溃，首断如斩 - Means 'the next day, a boil appeared on the neck like a millet seed, gradually spreading to all sides, and the head was severed as if cut off,' where '疽' means 'boil,' '粟颗' means 'millet seed,' '四溃' means 'spreading to all sides,' and '首断如斩' means 'the head was severed as if cut off.'
  • 是人所不能报，鬼亦报之矣 - Means 'this shows that even if humans cannot take revenge, ghosts can,' where '报' means 'to take revenge.'
  • 人之爱子谁不如我 - Means 'who loves their children more than I do,' where '爱子' means 'loved ones' and '谁不如我' means 'who is not like me.'
  • 其强者衔冤茹痛，郁结莫申，一决横流，势所必至 - Means 'those who are strong bear their grievances and pain, their pent-up anger cannot be expressed, and once released, it inevitably flows out,' where '衔冤茹痛' means 'bear grievances and pain,' '郁结莫申' means 'pent-up anger cannot be expressed,' and '一决横流，势所必至' means 'once released, it inevitably flows out.'
  • 其弱者横遭荼毒，赍恨黄泉，哀感三灵，岂无神理 - Means 'those who are weak suffer cruel treatment, carrying their hatred to the netherworld, their sorrow affecting the spirits, how can there be no divine justice,' where '横遭荼毒' means 'suffer cruel treatment,' '赍恨黄泉' means 'carry their hatred to the netherworld,' '哀感三灵' means 'their sorrow affects the spirits,' and '岂无神理' means 'how can there be no divine justice.'
  • 不有人祸，必有天刑，固亦理之自然耳 - Means 'if there is no human punishment, there must be divine retribution, which is also a natural law,' where '天刑' means 'divine retribution' and '理之自然耳' means 'which is also a natural law.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 笞 - A form of corporal punishment in ancient China.
  • 京师 - The capital city, referring to the imperial capital.
  • 群婢 - Refers to a group of maids or concubines.
  • 亲串 - Refers to relatives and acquaintances.
  • 黑气如车轮 - A supernatural phenomenon often associated with omens or divine intervention.
  • 三灵 - Refers to the three spirits or deities, often used in classical Chinese to denote the spiritual realm.
  • 天刑 - Divine punishment, reflecting the belief in a higher moral order.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '然无伤痕，其父讼不得直，反受笞，冤愤莫释，夜逾垣入，并其母女手刃之，缉捕多年，竟终漏网，是不为盗亦能报矣' describes a series of events: the father's lawsuit, the beating, the revenge, and the eventual escape. It must be read as a continuous narrative.
  • The sentence '又言京师某家火，夫妇子女并焚，亦群婢怨毒之所为，事无显证，遂无可追求，是不必有父，亦自能报矣' describes another incident involving a house fire and the subsequent lack of evidence, leading to the conclusion that even without a father, one can take revenge.
  • The sentence '余有亲串，鞭笞婢妾，嬉笑如儿戏，间有死者，一夕有黑气如车轮，自檐堕下，旋转如风，啾啾然有声，直入内室而隐' describes a series of events involving the mistreatment of maids and concubines and a supernatural occurrence.
  • The sentence '次日，疽发于项如粟颗，渐以四溃，首断如斩，是人所不能报，鬼亦报之矣' describes the consequences of the supernatural event and the idea that even humans cannot take revenge, but ghosts can.
  • The sentence '人之爱子谁不如我，其强者衔冤茹痛，郁结莫申，一决横流，势所必至；其弱者横遭荼毒，赍恨黄泉，哀感三灵，岂无神理？不有人祸，必有天刑，固亦理之自然耳' summarizes the theme of the passage, discussing the natural order of justice and retribution.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 闽人有女，未嫁卒，已葬矣 - A standard narrative opening in classical Chinese. '未嫁卒' means 'died before marriage.' The phrase '已葬矣' indicates that she was already buried.
  • 阅岁余 - Literally 'after more than a year,' used to denote a passage of time.
  • 亲串 - A term for a close relative or a relative by marriage; can also mean a family connection or acquaintance.
  • 出其不意，从后试呼其小名 - A colloquial expression meaning 'without warning, called her pet name from behind.'
  • 知不谬 - 'Knew it was not a mistake' or 'realized it was not an error.'
  • 疑为鬼 - 'Suspected it was a ghost,' a common trope in classical Chinese narratives.
  • 开冢验视果空棺 - 'Opened the tomb to inspect and found it empty,' a phrase used in legal or supernatural contexts.
  • 共往踪迹 - 'Together they went to trace the trail' or 'investigated the matter together.'
  • 初阳不相识 - 'At first pretended not to recognize her,' suggesting deception.
  • 举其胸肋瘢痣 - 'Pointed out the scars and moles on her chest and ribs,' used as identifying marks.
  • 呼邻妇密视 - 'Called in a neighbor's wife to look closely,' indicating a forensic or investigative step.
  • 乃具伏 - 'Then she confessed fully,' or 'she finally confessed.'
  • 觅其夫则已遁矣 - 'When they searched for her husband, he had already fled,' indicating flight or escape.
  • 盖闽中茉莉花根，以酒磨汁，饮之一寸，可尸噘一日 - 'It is said that in Fujian, the root of the jasmine flower, ground into juice with wine, when drunk in a quantity of one inch, can make a person appear dead for a day.'
  • 服至六寸尚可苏，至七寸乃真死 - 'If consumed in six inches, one can still revive; at seven inches, it becomes true death.' This is a dosage-based description of a toxic or anesthetic substance.
  • 女已有婿，而私与邻子狎 - 'The woman already had a husband, but secretly engaged in intimacy with a neighbor’s son.'
  • 故磨此根使诈死，待其葬而发墓共逃也 - 'Therefore, she ground this root to fake her death, waiting for her burial and then to dig up her body and flee together.'
  • 婿家鸣官捕得邻子，供词与女同 - 'The husband’s family reported to the authorities and captured the neighbor’s son; his testimony matched the woman’s.'
  • 时吴林塘官闽县，亲鞠是狱 - 'At that time, Wu Lintang was the magistrate of Min County and personally investigated the case.'
  • 欲引开棺见尸律，则人实未死，事异图财 - 'If we cite the law on opening a coffin to see the body, the person was not actually dead, so the case differs from theft or financial gain.'
  • 欲引药迷子女例，则女本同谋，情殊掠卖 - 'If we cite the law on drugging children, the woman was originally a co-conspirator, so the situation differs from abduction or selling.'
  • 无正条可以拟罪，乃仍以奸拐本律断 - 'There was no direct legal provision to apply, so they still judged according to the original law on adultery and abduction.'
  • 人情变幻，亦何所不有乎？ - 'Human nature is ever-changing; what strange things are not possible?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 闽人 - A person from Fujian province, a region in southeastern China.
  • 茉莉花根 - The root of the jasmine flower, a plant native to the region and used in traditional medicine or as a hallucinogenic or anesthetic substance.
  • 酒磨汁 - Grinding the root with wine to extract its juice, a method common in traditional Chinese herbal medicine.
  • 尸噘一日 - A term describing the state of 'appearing dead for a day' – a form of induced death or apparent death.
  • 服至六寸尚可苏，至七寸乃真死 - A dosage-based system, where '寸' (inch) is used as a measure of liquid, likely based on traditional Chinese medicine or alchemy.
  • 诈死 - 'Faking death,' a common motif in classical Chinese fiction and legal cases, often involving deception or escape.
  • 发墓 - 'Opening the tomb,' a serious crime in Chinese law, especially when the body is not yet decayed.
  • 奸拐 - A legal term referring to adultery and abduction, often used in classical Chinese legal codes.
  • 吴林塘 - A historical figure, likely a magistrate in Fujian during the Qing dynasty or earlier. His name may be fictional or based on a real person.
  • 闽县 - A county in Fujian province, possibly a historical or fictional location.

Proper Nouns

  • 闽人 - A person from Fujian province.
  • 吴林塘 - A magistrate in Min County (likely a historical or fictional name).
  • 闽县 - A county in Fujian province.

  • 四望天水相合处 - This phrase can be read as a single descriptive clause, emphasizing the vastness of the scene.

  • 日将近地平，则斜影横穿，未明先睹 - This sequence describes the sun's approach to the horizon and the resulting visual effects, which can be read as a continuous process.
  • 今所见者，是日之影，非日之形，是天上之日影隔水而映，非海中之日影浴水而出也 - This complex sentence distinguishes between the sun's reflection and its actual form, requiring careful parsing to maintain clarity.
  • 至日出地平，则影斜落海底，转不能见矣 - This final sequence describes the sun's position at sunrise and the resulting disappearance of the shadow, which can be read as a single observation or a series of events.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 果此心一举万物，即可以生乎？ - Rhetorical question: 'If this heart, by raising all things, can bring life into being, is that truly the case?'
  • 至大學條目，自格致以至治平，節節相因 - Classical phrase from the *Great Learning* referring to the structured progression of learning from investigating things to achieving peace in the world.
  • 節節各有其功力 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'each step has its own power or contribution.'
  • 譬如土生苗，苗成禾，禾成谷，谷成米，米成飯 - Metaphor illustrating a causal and sequential process.
  • 本節節相因 - Repetition of the previous idea, emphasizing that the process is sequential and interdependent.
  • 節節各有其功力 - Repetition to reinforce the idea that each stage in the process has its own distinct contribution.
  • 西山作大學衍義 - Reference to the work *Da Xue Yan Yi* (*The Elaboration of the Great Learning*), written by the Song dynasty scholar Zhu Xi (朱熹), who was known as *Xishan* (西山) in some contexts.
  • 列目至齊家而止 - Phrase meaning 'the categories listed only go up to managing the family.'
  • 謂治國平天下，可舉而措之 - Phrase meaning 'it is said that governing the state and pacifying the world can be carried out and applied directly.'

  • 抑猶有治法在乎？ - Rhetorical question: 'Or is there still a method of governance to be considered?'
  • 果太姒徽音而江漢即化，崇侯即服乎？ - Rhetorical question: 'Did Tai Si's virtuous influence cause the Jiang and Han rivers to be transformed, and the Chonghou to submit?'

  • 今一切棄置，而歸本于齊家，毋亦如土可生苗，即炊土為飯乎？ - Metaphorical expression: 'Now, all of this is being discarded, and the root is being traced back to managing the family—does this not resemble trying to cook soil into rice?'

  • 西山 - Likely refers to Zhu Xi (朱熹), a Song dynasty Neo-Confucian scholar, who was known by the title *Xishan* (西山) in some contexts.

  • 崇侯 - A notorious minister of the Shang dynasty, known for his corruption and opposition to King Wen of Zhou.

  • 要以三月齋戒乃能觀 - 'One must fast and purify for three months in order to see it.' Refers to the extreme difficulty of the task.

  • 第彼猶有棘刺，猶有母猴 - 'But in that case, there still is a thorn and a mother monkey.' Contrast with the current case.

  • 天下之至巧 - 'The most skillful thing in the world.'

  • 相與太息者久之 - 'They sighed together for a long time.'

  • 為是說者，其必永嘉之徒也夫 - 'Those who say this are surely followers of the Yongjia school.' *永嘉之徒* refers to scholars or thinkers from the Yongjia period, associated with a particular philosophical or literary tradition.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 言理言氣，言性言心 - These are classical philosophical categories from Confucian and Daoist thought. *理* (principle), *氣* (vital energy), *性* (nature), and *心* (mind) are central to Neo-Confucian philosophy, especially in the Song and Ming dynasties.
  • 先王之法 - 'The laws of the ancient kings,' referring to traditional Confucian ideals of governance and morality.
  • 王佐之才 - 'A talent fit to assist the king,' a term from Confucian political theory referring to a minister of exceptional ability.
  • 棘刺之端為母猴 - This is a reference to a famous classical fable or parable, often associated with the *Han Feizi* or other Warring States period texts. The idea is of an extremely difficult or impossible task, used to illustrate the absurdity of certain claims.
  • 三月齋戒 - A period of fasting and purification, common in religious and ritual contexts, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist practices.
  • 永嘉之徒 - The Yongjia period refers to the early 4th century during the Western Jin Dynasty, a time of political chaos and cultural transformation. *永嘉之徒* likely refers to scholars or thinkers from that period, possibly Neo-Confucian or Daoist, who emphasized abstract philosophy over practical matters.

Proper Nouns

  • 永嘉 - A period in Chinese history (307–420 CE) during the Western Jin Dynasty, marked by political instability and cultural change. *永嘉之徒* refers to scholars or thinkers from this era.

  • 母猴 - 'Mother monkey,' a metaphorical or symbolic figure in the fable.
  • 王佐 - 'Minister to the king,' a term from Confucian political theory.
  • 二士 - 'Two scholars' or 'two men of learning,' likely a generic term for learned individuals.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a recurring punishment and its effects: '今每至月朔，必受铁杖三十，未知何日得脱，此累累者皆是也'.
  • This sentence describes an attempt to speak further but being dragged away by spirits: '尚欲再言，已为鬼卒曳去'.
  • This sentence explains the reason for the actions taken: '多年伉俪，未免有情，故为营斋造福耳'.
  • This sentence explains the significance of the traditional wedding ceremony: '夫同牢之礼，于情最亲，亲则非疏者所能间'.
  • This sentence explains the principle of equality in marital relationships: '敌体之义，于分本尊，尊则非卑者所能违'.
  • This sentence explains the benefits of mutual understanding and cooperation: '故二人同心，则家庭之纤微曲折，男子所不能知，与知而不能自为者，皆足以弥缝其阙'.
  • This sentence explains the consequences of favoritism and manipulation: '苟徇其私爱，意有所偏，则机械百出，亦可于耳目所不及者，无所不为'.
  • This sentence explains the significant consequences of such actions: '种种衅端，种种败坏，皆从是起，所关者大，则其罪自不得轻'.
  • This sentence explains the severity of betrayal and its consequences: '况信之者至深，托之者至重，而欺其不觉，为所欲为，在朋友犹属负心，应干神谴，则人原一体，分属三纲者，其负心之罪，不更加倍蓰乎'.
  • This sentence explains the application of strict punishment for minor offenses: '寻常细故，断以严刑，因不得谓之深文矣'.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '後因事到鄉，诣其家，亦拒不见，知其相绝，乃大恨' describes a series of events: arriving in the village, visiting the house, being refused entry, realizing the estrangement, and feeling resentment.
  • The clause '会有挟妓诱博者讼于官，官断妓押归原籍，吏视之，乡民妇也' describes a legal case involving a courtesan and her return to her hometown, with the official's role in the process.
  • The clause '就与语。妇言苦为夫禁制，愧相负，相忆殊深，今幸相逢，乞念旧时数日欢，免杖免解' details the courtesan's plea to the official, explaining her situation and requesting leniency.
  • The final sequence '遣拘乡民，乡民携妻至，乃别一人，问乡里皆云不伪，问吏何以诬乡民，吏不能对。第曰：风闻。问闻之何人，则噤无语' describes the official's actions, the villagers' reactions, and the official's inability to provide a clear explanation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是夕 - Temporal marker indicating the time of an event; translated as 'That night'.
  • 果屋瓦乱鸣 - Descriptive phrase indicating unusual or supernatural activity; translated as 'The tiles of the house indeed made chaotic sounds'.
  • 窗扉震撼 - Vivid description of physical disturbance; translated as 'The windows and doors trembled'.
  • 妻党皆为砖石所击 - Indicating physical harm; translated as 'The wife's relatives were all struck by bricks and stones'.
  • 破额流血 - Describing the severity of the injuries; translated as 'Their foreheads were broken and they bled'.
  • 俄而 - Temporal marker indicating a short interval; translated as 'Soon after'.
  • 妻党妇女，并为狐媚 - Indicating supernatural influence; translated as 'The wife's relatives and women were all bewitched by foxes'.
  • 虽其母不免 - Emphasizing the universality of the affliction; translated as 'Even their mother was not spared'.
  • 昼则发狂裸走 - Describing a state of frenzy; translated as 'During the day, they went mad and walked naked'.
  • 丑词亵状 - Indicating inappropriate behavior; translated as 'Vulgar words and indecent actions'.
  • 无所不至 - Emphasizing the extent of the behavior; translated as 'They did everything'.
  • 夜则每室坌集数十狐 - Describing the supernatural activity; translated as 'At night, dozens of foxes would gather in each room'.
  • 更番嬲戏 - Indicating persistent harassment; translated as 'They would take turns teasing and playing'.
  • 不胜其创 - Emphasizing the severity of the injuries; translated as 'They could not bear the wounds'.
  • 哀乞声相闻 - Describing the emotional distress; translated as 'Their cries for mercy could be heard'.
  • 厨中肴馔，俱摄置太学父子前 - Indicating the supernatural theft; translated as 'The food in the kitchen was all taken and placed before the father and son of Taixue'.
  • 妻党所食皆杂以秽物 - Describing the contamination; translated as 'The wife's relatives' food was all mixed with filthy things'.
  • 知不可住，皆窜归 - Indicating the decision to leave; translated as 'Knowing they could not stay, they all fled back'.
  • 太学乃稍稍召集旧仆，复理家政 - Describing the restoration of order; translated as 'Taixue then gradually gathered his old servants and resumed household affairs'.
  • 始可以自存 - Indicating a return to normalcy; translated as 'He could finally survive on his own'.
  • 妻党觊觎未息，恒来探视 - Indicating persistent interference; translated as 'The wife's relatives' covetousness did not cease, and they always came to visit'.
  • 入门辄被击 - Describing the hostile reception; translated as 'As soon as they entered the door, they were struck'.
  • 或私有所携，归家则囊已空矣 - Indicating theft; translated as 'If they privately brought something, by the time they returned home, their bags were already empty'.
  • 其妻或私馈亦然 - Emphasizing the universality of the theft; translated as 'If his wife privately gave something, it was the same'.
  • 由是遂绝迹 - Indicating the cessation of interference; translated as 'Thus, they finally stopped coming'.
  • 然核计赀产损耗已甚 - Indicating the extent of the damage; translated as 'However, when calculating the loss of property, it was already severe'.
  • 微狐力，则太学父子饿殍矣 - Emphasizing the foxes' crucial role; translated as 'Without the foxes' help, the father and son of Taixue would have starved to death'.
  • 此至亲密友所不能代谋，此狐百计代谋之 - Indicating the foxes' exceptional help; translated as 'This is something even the closest friends could not do for them, but these foxes did it with all their efforts'.
  • 岂狐之果胜人哉 - Rhetorical question indicating skepticism; translated as 'Is it that the foxes are indeed superior to humans?'.
  • 人于世故深故远嫌畏怨，趋易避难，坐视而不救 - Describing human behavior; translated as 'People, being deeply involved in worldly affairs, avoid suspicion and fear resentment, seek ease and avoid difficulty, and sit by and watch without helping'.
  • 狐则未谙世故，故不巧博忠厚长者名 - Indicating the foxes' naivety; translated as 'The foxes, not being familiar with worldly affairs, are not skilled at gaining the reputation of being loyal and kind elders'.
  • 义所当为，奋然而起也 - Describing the foxes' moral actions; translated as 'When it is the right thing to do, they rise up with determination'.
  • 虽狐也，为之执鞭，所欣慕焉 - Indicating admiration for the foxes' actions; translated as 'Even if it is a fox, to hold its whip, that is what I admire'.

  • 狐媚 - Refers to the supernatural power of foxes in Chinese folklore, where they are often depicted as shape-shifters and tricksters; translated as 'Fox enchantment'.
  • 狐 - Refers to foxes, which in Chinese folklore are often associated with supernatural abilities and mischief, but can also be benevolent or protective; translated as 'Foxes'.
  • 世故 - Refers to the complexities and nuances of human society and social interactions; translated as 'Worldly affairs'.

  • 狐 - Foxes, which are central to the supernatural elements of the story; translated as 'Foxes'.

  • 竟共堕河中，随流而没 - '竟' adds an element of finality, and '随流而没' means 'drowned in the current.'
  • 桐捶其左胁骨尽断 - Describes the victim being beaten with a plank (桐) until his left ribs are broken.

  • 十指抠桐肩背，深入寸余 - Describes the blind man's fingers digging into the victim's shoulders and back, penetrating more than an inch.
  • 两颧两颊，啮肉几尽 - Describes the blind man biting the victim's cheeks and jaws until almost all the flesh is gone.
  • 疑必父母之冤也 - Suggests that the blind man might have been seeking revenge for his parents' wrongs.
  • 夫以无目之人，侦有目之人 - Compares the blind man's ability to track someone with eyesight, highlighting his extraordinary skill.
  • 如较伍胥之楚仇，其报更难矣 - Compares the blind man's pursuit to that of Wu Zixu, who sought revenge against the state of Chu, suggesting that such revenge is extremely difficult.
  • 竟卒得而食其肉 - Describes the blind man finally catching and eating the victim, a grim and symbolic act.
  • 岂非精诚之至，天地亦不能违乎 - Questions whether the blind man's intense sincerity and determination could not be defied by heaven and earth.
  • 宋高宗之歌舞湖山，究未可以势弱解也 - Critiques Emperor Gaozong of Song for his indulgence in pleasure, suggesting that his weakness cannot be excused by his position.

  • 宋儒精而昌黎粗 - Comparative phrase meaning 'the Song scholars are refined while Han Yu is coarse,' further contrasting the refinement of Song scholars with the apparent crudeness of Han Yu.
  • 披缁之徒 - Refers to Buddhist monks, literally 'those who wear the yellow robe.'
  • 畏昌黎不畏宋儒 - Means 'fear Han Yu but not the Song scholars,' indicating a specific attitude toward Han Yu and Song scholars.
  • 衔昌黎不衔宋儒 - Means 'resent Han Yu but not the Song scholars,' indicating a specific attitude toward Han Yu and Song scholars.
  • 檀施供养之佛 - Refers to the Buddha as an object of charity and worship, literally 'the Buddha of alms and offerings.'
  • 为愚夫妇言之 - Means 'spoken to the common people,' indicating the intended audience of Han Yu's critique.
  • 明心见性之佛 - Refers to the Buddha as an object of spiritual insight, literally 'the Buddha of enlightened mind and nature.'
  • 为士大夫言之 - Means 'spoken to the literati,' indicating the intended audience of the Song scholars' critique.
  • 天下士大夫少而愚夫妇多 - Comparative phrase meaning 'there are fewer literati than common people in the world,' highlighting the demographic distribution.
  • 僧徒之所取给 - Refers to the means of support for Buddhist monks, literally 'the means by which monks sustain themselves.'
  • 资于士大夫者少，资于愚夫妇者多 - Means 'relying on the literati is less, relying on the common people is more,' indicating the sources of support for monks.
  • 使昌黎之说胜 - Means 'if Han Yu's critique prevails,' introducing a conditional scenario.
  • 香积无烟，祗园无地 - Metaphorical expressions meaning 'no offerings would be made, no monasteries would exist,' indicating the potential consequences of Han Yu's critique.
  • 虽有大善知识 - Means 'even if there are great wise teachers,' introducing a hypothetical scenario.
  • 能率恒河沙众，枵腹露宿而说法哉 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'could they lead countless followers to preach with empty stomachs and exposed to the elements?' indicating the impracticality of the scenario.
  • 此如用兵者，先断粮道，不攻而自溃也 - Military metaphor meaning 'it is like a military commander who first cuts off the enemy's supply lines, causing them to collapse without a fight,' illustrating the effectiveness of Han Yu's critique.
  • 使宋儒之说胜 - Means 'if the Song scholars' critique prevails,' introducing another conditional scenario.
  • 尔儒理如是，儒法如是 - Means 'your Confucian principles are like this, your Confucian methods are like this,' indicating the content of the Song scholars' critique.
  • 尔不必从我，我佛理如是，佛法如是 - Means 'you do not need to follow me, my Buddhist principles are like this, my Buddhist methods are like this,' indicating the content of the Song scholars' critique.
  • 各尊所闻，各行所知 - Means 'each respects what they hear, each acts according to what they know,' indicating a state of mutual respect and independence.
  • 两相枝拄，未有害也 - Means 'they support each other, causing no harm,' indicating a harmonious relationship.
  • 故不畏宋儒，亦不甚衔宋儒 - Means 'therefore, they do not fear the Song scholars, nor do they greatly resent them,' indicating a neutral attitude toward the Song scholars.
  • 唐以前之儒 - Refers to scholars before the Tang dynasty.
  • 语语有实用 - Means 'every word has practical use,' indicating the practicality of pre-Tang scholars.
  • 宋以后之儒 - Refers to scholars after the Tang dynasty.
  • 事事皆空谈 - Means 'everything is empty talk,' indicating the lack of practicality of post-Tang scholars.
  • 讲学家之辟佛 - Refers to the critique of Buddhism by scholars, literally 'the critique of Buddhism by scholars.'
  • 于释氏毫无所加损 - Means 'it does not add or subtract anything from Buddhism,' indicating the neutrality of the critique.
  • 徒喧哄耳 - Means 'it is just noise,' indicating the futility of the critique.
  • 录以为功，固为党论 - Means 'if recorded as a contribution, it is indeed a partisan view,' indicating the bias of the critique.
  • 录以为罪，亦未免重视留良耳 - Means 'if recorded as a fault, it is also a matter of valuing Han Yu too highly,' indicating the bias of the critique.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宋清远先生言 - Means 'Mr. Song Qingyuan said,' introducing the narrative.
  • 昔在王坦斋先生学幕时 - Means 'when Mr. Wang Tanzhai was studying in the magistrate's office,' providing historical context.
  • 一友言梦游至冥司 - Means 'a friend said he dreamt of traveling to the underworld,' setting the scene for the story.
  • 见衣冠数十人累累入 - Means 'he saw dozens of people in official robes entering in a row,' describing the scene.
  • 冥王诘责良久 - Means 'the king of the underworld interrogated them for a long time,' indicating the nature of the interrogation.
  • 又累累出，各有愧恨之色 - Means 'they left in a row, each with a look of regret and anger,' describing the aftermath.
  • 偶见一吏，似相识而不记姓名 - Means 'he happened to see an official, who seemed familiar but whose name he couldn't remember,' introducing the encounter.
  • 试揖之，亦相答 - Means 'he tried to bow to him, and the official responded,' showing the interaction.
  • 此并何人，作此形状？ - Means 'Who are these people, and why do they look like this?' asking for an explanation.
  • 君亦居幕府 - Means 'You also work in the magistrate's office,' addressing the official.
  • 仆但两次佐学幕，未入有司署也 - Means 'I have only served twice in the magistrate's office and have never entered the government office,' explaining the narrator's experience.
  • 然则真不知矣 - Means 'Then I truly don't know,' indicating the official's response.
  • 此所谓四救先生者也 - Means 'These are the so-called 'Four Saviors' of Mr. Four,' identifying the group.
  • 问四救何义 - Means 'What does 'Four Saviors' mean?' asking for the definition.
  • 佐幕者有相传口诀 - Means 'Those who assist in the magistrate's office have a traditional saying,' introducing the saying.
  • 救生不救死，救官不救民，救大不救小，救旧不救新 - Means 'Save the living, not the dead; save officials, not the common people; save the important, not the minor; save the old, not the new,' explaining the saying.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 救生不救死者 - A moral principle that prioritizes saving the living over the dead.
  • 斷無可救 - Means 'there is no way to save' or 'it is impossible to save.'
  • 寧委曲以出之 - Means 'it is better to yield or make concessions to save them.'
  • 死者衔冤与否 - Refers to whether the dead person harbors a grudge or not.
  • 救官不救民者 - A moral principle that prioritizes saving officials over ordinary people.
  • 上控之案 - Refers to cases appealed to higher authorities.
  • 使冤得申 - Means 'if the injustice is redressed.'
  • 使不得申 - Means 'if the injustice is not redressed.'
  • 反坐 - Refers to the punishment for the official if the case is reversed.
  • 救大不救小者 - A moral principle that prioritizes saving higher-ranking officials over lower-ranking ones.
  • 罪归上官 - Means 'the crime is attributed to the higher-ranking official.'
  • 权位重者谴愈重 - Means 'those with greater power and position receive heavier punishment.'
  • 牵累必多 - Means 'many others will be implicated.'
  • 罪归微官 - Means 'the crime is attributed to the lower-ranking official.'
  • 责任轻者罚可轻 - Means 'those with lesser responsibility receive lighter punishment.'
  • 归结较易 - Means 'it is easier to conclude the case.'
  • 救旧不救新者 - A moral principle that prioritizes saving old officials over new ones.
  • 旧官已去 - Means 'the old official has left office.'
  • 有所未了 - Means 'there are unresolved matters.'
  • 羁留之恐不能偿 - Means 'if they are detained, they may not be able to pay.'
  • 新官方来 - Means 'the new official has just arrived.'
  • 有所委卸 - Means 'there are matters to be passed on.'
  • 强抑之尚可以办 - Means 'forcing them to stay can still be managed.'
  • 新官之能堪与否 - Refers to whether the new official can handle the situation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 救生不救死者 - Reflects a moral principle common in Confucian thought, emphasizing the value of life.
  • 救官不救民者 - Reflects a societal hierarchy where officials are often given priority over common people.
  • 救大不救小者 - Reflects a hierarchical system where higher-ranking officials are given more leniency.
  • 救旧不救新者 - Reflects a preference for established officials over newcomers, possibly due to their experience and connections.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 救生不救死者，死者已死，断无可救，生者尚生，又杀以抵命，是多死一人也 - Emphasizes the moral dilemma of choosing between saving the living and the dead.
  • 宁委曲以出之，而死者衔冤与否，则非所计也 - Highlights the prioritization of saving the living over the dead, even if it means the dead may harbor a grudge.
  • 救官不救民者，上控之案，使冤得申，则官之祸福不可测，使不得申，即反坐，不过军流耳 - Outlines the potential outcomes of appealing cases and the varying degrees of punishment.
  • 救大不救小者，罪归上官，则权位重者谴愈重，且牵累必多，罪归微官，则责任轻者罚可轻，且归结较易 - Compares the consequences of attributing crimes to higher versus lower-ranking officials.
  • 救旧不救新者，旧官已去，有所未了，羁留之恐不能偿，新官方来，有所委卸，强抑之尚可以办，其新官之能堪与否，则非所计也 - Discusses the implications of detaining old officials versus new ones.

  • 恩仇私相報復 - Refers to personal grudges and revenge, often driven by private emotions rather than justice.

  • 莫明其入梦之故 - Means to be unable to understand the reason for entering a dream, often used to describe a mysterious or inexplicable event.
  • 神明或假告人 - Refers to the possibility that a divine or supernatural force is guiding or warning someone.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 从伯君章公 - A title or honorific, likely referring to a respected elder or senior figure.
  • 月夕纳凉 - A classical phrase meaning 'to cool off on a moonlit night.'
  • 长揖 - A formal bow, often used as a greeting or sign of respect.
  • 仆不幸获谴于社公 - I, unfortunately, have been punished by the village deity (社公). This phrase reflects the belief in village gods and their influence.
  • 自祷弗解也 - I have prayed but to no avail. Indicates a sense of futility in personal prayer.
  • 一社之中，惟君祀社公最丰 - In the village, only you worship the village deity most abundantly. Highlights the importance of the subject's worship.
  • 数十年一无所祈请 - For decades, you have made no requests or petitions. Suggests a pattern of self-restraint in religious practice.
  • 社公甚德君，亦甚重君 - The village deity greatly appreciates and values you. Emphasizes the deity's positive regard.
  • 君为一祷，必见从 - If you pray for me, the deity will surely listen. Implies a reciprocal relationship between the deity and the worshiper.
  • 尔何人 - Who are you? A direct question to the stranger.
  • 某故诸生，与君先人亦相识 - I was once a scholar, and I knew your ancestor. Provides background on the stranger's identity.
  • 今下世三十余年矣 - I have been dead for over thirty years. Indicates the stranger's status as a ghost or spirit.
  • 昨偶向某家索食，为所诉也 - Yesterday, I happened to ask for food from a certain house, and they complained. Explains the reason for the punishment.
  • 己事不祈请，乃祈请人事乎 - If you do not pray for your own affairs, why pray for others'? A rhetorical question challenging the stranger's request.
  • 人事不祈请，乃祈请鬼事乎 - If you do not pray for human affairs, why pray for ghostly matters? Another rhetorical question emphasizing the subject's principled stance.
  • 仆无能为役，先生休矣 - I am unable to serve you, sir, so please rest. A polite refusal to help.
  • 掉臂去曰 - He left with a wave of his arm, saying... Describes the stranger's departure.
  • 自了汉耳，不足谋也 - I am a self-reliant man; you are not worth my trouble. Indicates the stranger's disdain.
  • 肴酒必丰，敬鬼神也 - Food and wine must be abundant, showing respect to the spirits. Emphasizes the importance of proper offerings.
  • 无所祈请，远之也 - Making no requests, distancing oneself from the spirits. Suggests a policy of non-interference.
  • 敬鬼神而远之 - Respecting the spirits but keeping a distance. A key Confucian principle.
  • 即民之义也 - This is the way of the people. Suggests a practical and ethical approach.
  • 视流俗之谄渎，迂儒之傲侮，为得其中矣 - Compared to the flattery and disrespect of the common people and the arrogance and contempt of the scholars, this is the middle way. Evaluates the subject's approach as balanced.
  • 高山仰止，景行行止 - The high mountains we look up to, the bright paths we follow. A classical phrase praising someone's moral excellence.
  • 俯仰七十年间，能勿睪然远想哉 - In the span of seventy years, can we not look back with admiration? Reflects on the passage of time and the subject's enduring influence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 社公 - Refers to the village deity, a local god worshipped in rural areas.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 奴子傅显喜读书，颇知文义，亦稍知医药，性情迂缓，望之如偃蹇老儒。 - Describes the character Fu Xian and his characteristics. '奴子' refers to a servant, '颇知文义' means has some knowledge of literature, '亦稍知医药' means also has some knowledge of medicine, '性情迂缓' describes a slow and deliberate personality, '望之如偃蹇老儒' means looks like a lazy old scholar.
  • 一日，雅步行市上，逢人辄问见魏三兄否——奴子魏藻行三也，或指所在，复雅步以往。 - Describes Fu Xian's actions on a particular day. '雅步' means walks gracefully, '逢人辄问' means asks everyone if they have seen Brother Wei San, '奴子魏藻行三也' is a parenthetical explanation of '魏三兄' (Brother Wei San), '或指所在' means points to where he is, '复雅步以往' means then walks gracefully towards him.
  • 比相见，喘息良久，魏问相见何意？曰：适在苦水井前，遇见三嫂在树下作针黹，倦而假寝，小儿嬉戏井旁，相距三五尺耳。似乎可虑，男女有别，不便呼三嫂使醒，故走觅兄。 - Describes the meeting between Fu Xian and Wei San. '比相见' means when they meet, '喘息良久' means breathes heavily for a long time, '魏问相见何意' means Wei asks what the purpose of the meeting is, '适在苦水井前' means just happened to be at the well of bitter water, '遇见三嫂在树下作针黹' means met Sister-in-law Wei San working on embroidery under a tree, '倦而假寝' means tired and took a nap, '小儿嬉戏井旁' means a child was playing near the well, '相距三五尺耳' means only three or five feet away, '似乎可虑' means seems concerning, '男女有别' means men and women have their distinctions, '不便呼三嫂使醒' means it was inconvenient to call Sister-in-law Wei San to wake up, '故走觅兄' means so went to find Brother Wei San.
  • 魏大骇，奔往，则妇已俯井哭子矣。 - Describes Wei San's reaction and the outcome of the situation. '魏大骇' means Wei was greatly startled, '奔往' means ran towards her, '则妇已俯井哭子矣' means and the woman had already fallen into the well crying for her child.
  • 夫僮仆读书，可云佳事，然读书以明理，明理以致用也。食而不化至昏愦僻谬，贻害无穷，亦何贵此儒者哉。 - Provides a moral lesson about the value of reading and the dangers of superficial learning. '夫僮仆读书' means indeed, servants reading books, '可云佳事' means can be called a good thing, '然读书以明理' means however, reading books is to understand principles, '明理以致用也' means understanding principles to apply them, '食而不化至昏愦僻谬' means eating without digesting to the point of confusion and absurdity, '贻害无穷' means causing endless harm, '亦何贵此儒者哉' means why value such scholars?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 奴子 - Refers to a servant or slave, which was a common social class in ancient China.
  • 文义 - Refers to the meaning or interpretation of literature, reflecting the importance of literary studies in ancient Chinese culture.
  • 医药 - Refers to medicine, indicating the value placed on medical knowledge in ancient China.
  • 性情迂缓 - Describes a person who is slow and deliberate, which was often seen as a virtue in Confucian thought.
  • 偃蹇老儒 - Refers to a lazy old scholar, which can be seen as a critique of those who are overly scholarly but lack practical application.
  • 雅步 - Refers to a graceful walk, which is a sign of refinement and good manners in Chinese culture.
  • 奴子魏藻行三也 - Indicates the naming convention where '行三' (third in line) is a common way to denote the order of birth among siblings.
  • 男女有别 - Refers to the traditional Chinese belief that men and women have distinct roles and behaviors, which is deeply rooted in Confucian ethics.
  • 食而不化 - A metaphor for absorbing knowledge without understanding or applying it, leading to confusion and absurdity.
  • 贻害无穷 - Refers to causing endless harm, which is a serious criticism in Chinese culture.
  • 儒者 - Refers to scholars or Confucian scholars, who were highly respected in ancient China for their knowledge and moral integrity.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 余于四库全书总目已详论之 - Formal statement meaning 'I have already discussed this in detail in the General Catalogue of the Four Treasuries.'
  • 揣骨亦莫明所自起 - Phrase meaning 'even bone divination cannot clearly explain its origin,' indicating the mysterious nature of the practice.
  • 考太平广记一百三十六引三国典略 - Reference to consulting the *Taiping Guangji* (A General Record of the Taiping Era), specifically volume 136, which cites *The Annals of the Three Kingdoms*.
  • 似此术南北朝已有 - Phrase meaning 'it seems this practice already existed in the Northern and Southern Dynasties,' indicating the antiquity of the practice.
  • 流传既古，当有所受 - Phrase meaning 'since it has been passed down for so long, it must have some origin,' suggesting the practice has a historical basis.
  • 一知半解，往往或中 - Phrase meaning 'even a partial understanding can sometimes hit the mark,' indicating the potential effectiveness of the practice despite its limitations.
  • 较太素脉稍有据耳 - Phrase meaning 'it is slightly more grounded than the *Tai Su Mai* (a form of pulse diagnosis),' suggesting a comparative evaluation of the practice.
  • 诚谋英勇公阿公言——文成公之子，袭封 - Formal statement introducing the speaker as 'the grandson of the Duke of Chengmou, who was the son of the Duke of Wencheng and inherited the title.'
  • 灯市口东，有二郎神庙 - Phrase meaning 'east of the Lantern Market, there is a Temple of the Second Lord,' introducing the location of the temple.
  • 其庙面西，而晓日初出，辄有金光射室中 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the temple faces west, and when the morning sun rises, it often emits golden light into the room,' describing the temple's orientation and the phenomenon observed.
  • 其邻屋则不然，莫喻其故 - Phrase meaning 'the neighboring houses do not experience this, and the reason is unclear,' indicating the uniqueness of the phenomenon.
  • 或曰：是庙基址与中和殿东西相直 - Phrase meaning 'some say that the temple's foundation is aligned east-west with the Hall of Central Harmony,' providing a possible explanation for the phenomenon.
  • 殿上火珠——宫殿金顶古谓之火珠 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the fire pearl on the palace roof—anciently, the golden-topped palaces were called fire pearls,' explaining the term 'fire pearl.'
  • 唐崔曙有明堂火珠诗是也 - Reference to a poem by Cui Shu from the Tang dynasty, which mentions the 'fire pearl' in the Hall of Supreme Harmony.
  • 其或然欤 - Phrase meaning 'perhaps this is the case,' expressing uncertainty about the explanation.

[1158]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 阿公偶问余刑天干戚事 - An elder casually asks about the story of Xingtian and his weapons.
  • 余举山海经以对 - The narrator cites the 'Classic of Mountains and Seas' to respond.
  • 君勿谓古记荒唐，是诚有也 - Do not say ancient records are absurd; they are truly real.
  • 昔科尔沁台吉达尔玛达都，尝猎于漠北深山 - In the past, the historical figure Taiji Dalima Dada hunted in the deep mountains of the northern desert.
  • 遇一鹿负箭而奔，因引弧殪之 - Encountered a deer carrying an arrow and ran, then drew a bow and killed it.
  • 方欲收取，忽一骑驰而至 - Just about to collect it, suddenly a rider galloped up.
  • 鞍上人有身无首，其目在两乳，其口在脐 - The person on the saddle had a body but no head, eyes on the nipples, and a mouth on the navel.
  • 语啁哳自脐出，虽不可辨，然观其手所指画 - Speech chattered out of the navel, though not understandable, but observed through hand gestures.
  • 似言鹿其所射，不应夺之也 - It seemed to say the deer he shot, it should not be taken away.
  • 从骑皆震慑失次，台吉素有胆，亦指画示以彼射未仆，此箭乃获 - The followers were all shocked and disordered, but the historical figure, being usually brave, also indicated with gestures that he did not shoot it down, this arrow was obtained.
  • 当剖而均分 - Should be split and divided equally.
  • 其人会意，亦似首肯，竟持半鹿而去 - The person understood, also nodding in agreement, finally took half the deer and left.
  • 不知其是何部族，居于何地，据其形状，岂非刑天之遗类欤 - Not knowing what tribe it is, where it lives, according to its appearance, isn't it a descendant of Xingtian?
  • 天地之大，何所不有，儒者自拘于见闻耳 - The vastness of heaven and earth, nothing is impossible, Confucian scholars are limited by their own knowledge.
  • 案史讫称山海经禹本纪，所有怪物，余不敢信 - According to history, the 'Classic of Mountains and Seas' and the 'Chronicle of Yu' contain all the monsters, I do not dare to believe.
  • 是其书本在汉以前，列子称大禹行而见之，伯益知而名之，夷坚闻而志之 - The book was originally written before the Han dynasty, the *Liezi* says Emperor Yu saw it, Bo Yi knew and named it, Yi Jian heard and recorded it.
  • 其言必有所受，特后人不免附益，又窜乱之，故往往悠谬太甚，且杂以秦汉之地名，分别观之可矣 - His words must have a basis, but later people added to it, and distorted it, so it is often too far-fetched, mixed with place names from the Qin and Han dynasties, it can be viewed separately.
  • 必谓本依附天问作山海经，不应引山海经，反注天问，则太过也 - It must be said that the *Shanhaijing* was originally based on *Tianwen*, it should not cite the *Shanhaijing* to annotate *Tianwen*, that is too much.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

[1159]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 能视鬼 - Literally means 'can see ghosts'; indicates a supernatural ability.
  • 质如烟雾 - Literally means 'substance like smoke and fog'; describes the hazy, insubstantial quality of ghosts.
  • 新鬼大，故鬼小 - A concept from the *Zuo Zhuan* suggesting that new ghosts are large, while old ghosts are small.
  • 气有厚薄，斯色有浓淡 - A philosophical concept suggesting that the thickness of qi (vital energy) affects the color and size of ghosts.
